bytes.topic: Data Exploration — profiling datasets, understanding distributions, missing values
bytes.title: Matplotlib subplots for Quick Dataset Profiling — Distributions and Missing Values in One Figure
bytes.body:
A single `plt.subplots()` call can give you a full dataset health check. Pair `ax.hist()` for distribution shape with `ax.barh()` for missing-value counts, and you instantly spot skew, outliers, and data gaps without leaving your notebook.

Use `fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows, 2)` to create a grid: left column for histograms of each numeric feature, right column for a single horizontal bar chart of null counts. Call `ax.hist(data, bins='sturges')` — the `bins` parameter matters more than people think. `'sturges'` works for normal-ish data; switch to `'fd'` (Freedman-Diaconis) for skewed columns to avoid hiding bimodality.

For missing values, `ax.barh()` with a colormap from `plt.cm.Reds` scaled by missingness percentage makes gaps pop visually. Add `ax.axvline()` at a threshold (e.g., 50%) to flag columns worth dropping.

Call `fig.tight_layout()` at the end — without it, axis labels overlap and the profiling view becomes unreadable. This two-panel pattern replaces dozens of scattered `.describe()` calls with one glanceable figure.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame(np.random.randn(500, 4), columns=list("ABCD"))
df.loc[df.sample(frac=0.3).index, "B"] = np.nan

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].hist(df["A"].dropna(), bins="fd", edgecolor="black", alpha=0.7)
axes[0].set_title("Distribution of A")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Value")

missing = df.isnull().sum().sort_values()
colors = plt.cm.Reds(missing / len(df))
axes[1].barh(missing.index, missing.values, color=colors)
axes[1].axvline(x=len(df) * 0.5, color="red", ls="--", label="50% threshold")
axes[1].set_title("Missing Values")
axes[1].legend()

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Pandas Fundamentals — DataFrames, Series, indexing, groupby, merge/join
bytes.title: matplotlib + Pandas groupby — Plot Aggregated DataFrames in One Call
bytes.body:
Pandas DataFrames integrate directly with matplotlib's plotting API via `.plot()`, but the real power shows when you combine `groupby` aggregations with visualization. Instead of manually looping through groups and calling `ax.bar()` repeatedly, pass grouped results straight into matplotlib-aware plot methods.

When you call `df.groupby('category')['value'].mean().plot(kind='bar')`, Pandas delegates to `matplotlib.pyplot` under the hood, setting tick labels from the index and values from the Series. For multi-column aggregations, `df.groupby('region')[['sales','returns']].sum().plot(kind='bar')` produces grouped bars automatically — matplotlib creates one bar cluster per index label.

For finer control, unstack a MultiIndex groupby into columns first: `df.groupby(['year','quarter'])['revenue'].sum().unstack().plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, ax=ax)`. This lets matplotlib's `Axes.bar` handle stacking natively. Always pass an explicit `ax` when embedding in subplots — relying on the implicit `pyplot` state machine leads to figure leaks in loops.
bytes.code_snippet:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'region': ['East','East','West','West','East','West'],
    'quarter': ['Q1','Q2','Q1','Q2','Q1','Q2'],
    'sales': [120, 150, 90, 200, 130, 170]
})

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

df.groupby('region')['sales'].sum().plot(kind='bar', ax=ax1, title='Sales by Region')

df.groupby(['region','quarter'])['sales'].sum().unstack().plot(
    kind='bar', stacked=True, ax=ax2, title='Stacked by Quarter')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

bytes.topic: NumPy — array operations, broadcasting, vectorization vs loops, memory layout
bytes.title: Matplotlib + NumPy Vectorization — Plot 1M Points Without the Loop Tax
bytes.body:
Matplotlib bindstightly to NumPy's memory layout. When you pass a Python list to plt.plot(), it internally converts to an ndarray — so you pay the conversion cost on every draw call. Pre-vectorizing your data eliminates this overhead and unlocks broadcasting for transforms.

C-contiguous arrays (row-major, NumPy's default) align with how Matplotlib's Agg backend reads vertex buffers. Passing a Fortran-ordered array forces a copy. Check with arr.flags['C_CONTIGUOUS'] before plotting large datasets.

Broadcasting shines when generating plot data. Instead of looping to compute y-values for multiple parameter curves, broadcast a column vector of x against a row vector of parameters — one expression, one allocation, one plot call via plt.plot(x, y_matrix). Matplotlib interprets each column as a separate series automatically.

For scatter plots with 100k+ points, vectorized color mapping via plt.cm.viridis(normalized_array) is ~50x faster than per-point color assignment. Always normalize with Normalize() before mapping.
bytes.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(0, 4 * np.pi, 500)
freqs = np.array([[1, 2, 3, 5]])        # shape (1,4) — broadcast target
y = np.sin(x[:, np.newaxis] * freqs)     # shape (500,4) — vectorized, no loop

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, y)                            # each column drawn as a series
ax.set_title('Broadcasted sine curves')
ax.legend([f'{f}x' for f in freqs.flat])
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Polars — lazy evaluation, expressions API, performance vs Pandas, when to switch
bytes.title: matplotlib + Polars — Plotting DataFrames Without Converting to Pandas
bytes.body:
Polars DataFrames don't plug into matplotlib directly — plt.plot() expects NumPy arrays or Pandas Series. But converting is trivial, and the payoff is huge: Polars' lazy evaluation and expressions API let you filter, aggregate, and reshape millions of rows 5–10× faster than Pandas before you ever call plt.subplots().

The key pattern: build your entire data pipeline in Polars using lazy frames (.lazy() → .filter() → .group_by() → .agg() → .collect()), then extract columns as NumPy arrays via .to_numpy() for matplotlib. This avoids the Pandas intermediate entirely.

Polars expressions like pl.col('latency').mean().over('service') replace verbose Pandas groupby-transform chains in a single readable line. When your plotting script spends 80% of its time in data prep and 20% in rendering, Polars cuts total runtime dramatically.

Switch when: datasets exceed 1M rows, you chain 3+ transformations before plotting, or your notebook cells are bottlenecked on .groupby().agg() — not on plt.savefig().
bytes.code_snippet:
import polars as pl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Lazy pipeline — no work happens until .collect()
result = (
    pl.scan_csv("metrics.csv")
    .filter(pl.col("status") == 200)
    .group_by("endpoint")
    .agg(pl.col("latency_ms").mean().alias("avg_latency"))
    .sort("avg_latency", descending=True)
    .head(10)
    .collect()
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.barh(result["endpoint"].to_numpy(), result["avg_latency"].to_numpy())
ax.set_xlabel("Avg Latency (ms)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("top_endpoints.png", dpi=150)

bytes.topic: Data Cleaning — handling nulls, outlier detection, type coercion, deduplication
bytes.title: Matplotlib — Visualizing Dirty Data Before You Clean It
bytes.body:
Matplotlib is your first line of defense in data cleaning. Before writing a single fillna() or drop_duplicates(), plot your data to understand what's actually wrong.

Use scatter plots to flag outliers — points that cluster far from the main distribution jump out immediately. Box plots via ax.boxplot() expose quartile spread and whisker-based outliers without manual IQR calculations. For null detection, ax.imshow() on a boolean null mask (df.isnull().values) renders a heatmap of missing data patterns — revealing whether gaps are random or structural (e.g., entire columns missing after a date).

Type coercion issues surface when plotting throws TypeError or produces flat lines where curves are expected — a signal that string-encoded numbers snuck in. Duplicates show as overlapping markers; setting alpha=0.3 in scatter() makes stacked points visibly darker.

The key insight: matplotlib doesn't clean your data, but it tells you exactly where and how it's dirty. Always plot before and after cleaning to verify your transformations didn't introduce new artifacts.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

data = np.random.randn(200)
data[::15] = np.nan
data[10], data[50] = 12.5, -9.8  # inject outliers

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 4))

axes[0].boxplot(data[~np.isnan(data)])
axes[0].set_title('Outlier Detection')

axes[1].imshow(np.isnan(data).reshape(10, 20), aspect='auto', cmap='Reds')
axes[1].set_title('Null Heatmap')

axes[2].scatter(range(len(data)), data, alpha=0.3, c='steelblue')
axes[2].set_title('Raw Distribution')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Feature Engineering — encoding categoricals, scaling, binning, interaction features
bytes.title: Matplotlib — Visualizing Feature Engineering Transforms Side by Side
bytes.body: Feature engineering transforms are only as good as your ability to inspect them. Matplotlib's subplot grid makes it trivial to compare raw vs. engineered features in a single figure. Use `fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3)` to lay out before-and-after histograms for scaling, binning, and encoded categoricals. For one-hot encoded columns, `axes[0].bar()` with category counts beats a histogram. For min-max scaled numerics, overlay the original distribution against the scaled one on `axes[1]` using twin y-axes via `axes[1].twinx()`. For binned features, `axes[2].hist()` with the `bins` parameter matching your `pd.cut()` edges ensures the visual aligns with your actual bin boundaries. Color-code transforms consistently — raw in `#1f77b4`, engineered in `#ff7f0e` — and call `ax.set_title()` per panel so each transform is instantly identifiable. Adding `ax.axvline()` at bin edges or scaling bounds gives immediate visual validation that your pipeline isn't clipping or collapsing variance.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
raw = np.random.exponential(scale=2.0, size=500)
scaled = (raw - raw.min()) / (raw.max() - raw.min())
bins_edges = [0, 1, 3, 6, raw.max()]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 4))
axes[0].hist(raw, bins=30, color='#1f77b4', edgecolor='black')
axes[0].set_title('Raw Distribution')
axes[1].hist(scaled, bins=30, color='#ff7f0e', edgecolor='black')
axes[1].set_title('Min-Max Scaled')
axes[2].hist(raw, bins=bins_edges, color='#2ca02c', edgecolor='black')
for edge in bins_edges:
    axes[2].axvline(edge, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=0.8)
axes[2].set_title('Custom Binned')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Exploratory Data Analysis — correlation analysis, pivot tables, aggregation patterns
bytes.title: Matplotlib heatmap() — Visualizing Correlation Matrices Without Seaborn
bytes.body: Most developers reach for seaborn's heatmap when visualizing correlations, but matplotlib's imshow() handles it natively with more control. Compute your correlation matrix with pandas, then render it using plt.imshow() with a diverging colormap like 'RdBu_r' centered at zero. The trick is pairing imshow() with plt.colorbar() and manually setting tick labels via ax.set_xticks() and ax.set_yticks() — this gives you full control over label rotation, font size, and grid alignment. For aggregation patterns like pivot tables, plot the pivoted DataFrame directly: pivot.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, ax=ax) works, but imshow() on pivot.values produces cleaner heatmaps for dense tables. Use ax.text() to annotate individual cells with correlation coefficients — loop over the matrix with nested range() calls and format to two decimals. Set aspect='auto' when your matrix isn't square. Use vmin=-1, vmax=1 to lock the color scale across comparable charts.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame(np.random.randn(100, 4), columns=['A', 'B', 'C', 'D'])
corr = df.corr()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))
im = ax.imshow(corr, cmap='RdBu_r', vmin=-1, vmax=1, aspect='auto')
ax.set_xticks(range(len(corr))), ax.set_xticklabels(corr.columns, rotation=45)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(corr))), ax.set_yticklabels(corr.columns)
for i in range(len(corr)):
    for j in range(len(corr)):
        ax.text(j, i, f'{corr.iloc[i, j]:.2f}', ha='center', va='center', fontsize=9)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label='Correlation')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Matplotlib — figure/axes API, customization, subplots, publication-quality charts
bytes.title: Matplotlib — Master the Figure/Axes API for Publication-Quality Charts
bytes.body:
The pyplot state machine (plt.plot()) works for quick plots but fights you at scale. Switch to the object-oriented Figure/Axes API: fig, ax = plt.subplots(). Every element — ticks, spines, legends — lives on the Axes object, giving you explicit control.

For multi-panel figures, plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8, 6)) returns a 2D array of Axes. Use constrained_layout=True instead of tight_layout() — it handles colorbars and shared labels without overlap.

Publication tricks: set ax.spines[['top','right']].set_visible(False) to remove chart junk. Use ax.tick_params(direction='in', length=4) for journal-style inward ticks. Set plt.rcParams['font.family'] = 'serif' globally or pass fontdict per call.

For consistent styling across figures, define a custom rcParams dict or use plt.style.use() with a .mplstyle file. Export with fig.savefig('plot.pdf', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight') — PDF preserves vector quality, and bbox_inches='tight' clips whitespace.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4), constrained_layout=True)

ax1.plot(x, np.sin(x), color='#2563eb', linewidth=1.5)
ax1.set_title('Sine', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax1.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
ax1.tick_params(direction='in', length=4)

ax2.plot(x, np.cos(x), color='#dc2626', linewidth=1.5)
ax2.set_title('Cosine', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax2.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
ax2.tick_params(direction='in', length=4)

fig.savefig('trig.pdf', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')

bytes.topic: Seaborn — statistical visualizations, themes, integration with Pandas
bytes.title: Matplotlib + Seaborn — Supercharge Statistical Plots Without Leaving pyplot
bytes.body: Seaborn builds directly on top of matplotlib's Figure and Axes objects, meaning every seaborn plot is a matplotlib plot you can customize further. Call sns.set_theme() to globally override matplotlib's rcParams — fonts, grid styles, and color palettes all flow through matplotlib's rendering engine. The real power is the return value: functions like sns.histplot() and sns.boxplot() return matplotlib Axes objects, so you can chain .set_xlabel(), .set_title(), or overlay additional plt.axhline() calls seamlessly. For Pandas DataFrames, seaborn accepts column names as strings via the data= parameter, eliminating manual array extraction. Use sns.FacetGrid to generate subplot grids that map to matplotlib's plt.subplots() under the hood — call .fig.suptitle() to add a figure-level title. When you need fine control, access the underlying Figure with plt.gcf() after any seaborn call. This interop means you never hit a wall: seaborn handles the statistics, matplotlib handles the pixel-perfect tweaks.
bytes.code_snippet:
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"category": ["A"]*50 + ["B"]*50,
                   "value": list(range(50)) + list(range(10, 60))})

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="muted")
ax = sns.boxplot(data=df, x="category", y="value")
ax.set_title("Distribution by Category", fontsize=14)
ax.axhline(y=30, color="red", linestyle="--", label="threshold")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Jupyter — notebook best practices, reproducibility, parameterized notebooks
bytes.title: Matplotlib in Jupyter — Reproducible Plots with Parameterized Notebooks
bytes.body:
Jupyter notebooks with matplotlib plots are notoriously hard to reproduce. Figures render differently across sessions when global state leaks between cells. Fix this by isolating each plot in its own figure context and pinning your style configuration at the notebook top.

Use `%matplotlib inline` with explicit `plt.figure()` calls instead of relying on implicit figure creation. For parameterized notebooks via Papermill, pass plot parameters (figsize, DPI, color palettes) as tagged cells so CI pipelines generate consistent visual outputs.

The key pattern: set `plt.rcParams` in a single setup cell, create every figure with `plt.subplots()` for explicit axes ownership, and call `fig.savefig()` with `bbox_inches='tight'` before `plt.show()`. This ensures your saved artifacts match what renders in-notebook.

For deterministic style across environments, use `plt.style.use()` with a checked-in `.mplstyle` file rather than inline `rcParams` scattered across cells. Papermill can then sweep parameters like date ranges while your plot styling stays version-controlled and reproducible.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import papermill as pm

# Tagged 'parameters' cell for Papermill injection
figsize = (10, 6)
dpi = 150
style_sheet = './house_style.mplstyle'

plt.style.use(style_sheet)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=figsize, dpi=dpi)
ax.plot([1, 2, 3], [1, 4, 9], linewidth=2)
ax.set_title('Parameterized Plot')
fig.savefig('output.png', bbox_inches='tight', dpi=dpi)
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Performance — vectorization, chunking large files, memory-efficient operations
bytes.title: Matplotlib — Plot Millions of Points Without Killing Your RAM
bytes.body:
Rendering large datasets in matplotlib grinds to a halt because each Artist object carries overhead. The fix: downsample before plotting, not after.

For scatter plots with 1M+ points, replace `ax.scatter()` with `ax.plot()` using marker-only style — `scatter` creates individual PathCollection entries while `plot` batches into a single Line2D. That alone cuts render time 5–10x.

For truly massive data, use `mpl_toolkits.mplot3d` sparingly and lean on rasterization. Setting `ax.plot(..., rasterized=True)` renders that artist as a bitmap embedded in the vector output. Your SVG/PDF stays small while the visual stays sharp.

Chunking matters for time-series. Instead of loading a full CSV into memory, read in chunks with pandas and call `ax.plot()` per chunk — matplotlib composites overlapping Line2D objects correctly. Combine with `ax.set_rasterization_zorder(0)` to rasterize everything below a z-order threshold.

Finally, use `plt.style.use('fast')` — it disables anti-aliasing and simplifies path rendering, cutting draw time by ~30%.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

plt.style.use('fast')
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

# 2M points: use plot() not scatter()
x = np.random.randn(2_000_000)
y = np.random.randn(2_000_000)

ax.plot(x, y, ',', alpha=0.1, rasterized=True)
ax.set_title('2M points — rasterized, fast style')
fig.savefig('large_plot.pdf', dpi=150)
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Time Series — resampling, rolling windows, lag features, seasonality
bytes.title: Matplotlib Time Series — Resampling, Rolling Windows, and Seasonality in One Plot
bytes.body: Matplotlib pairs naturally with pandas for time series visualization, but the key is layering resampled data, rolling statistics, and seasonal patterns into a single readable figure. Use `ax.plot()` on a DatetimeIndex-based Series for automatic tick formatting via `mdates`. Plot raw data with low alpha, overlay a rolling mean using `Series.rolling().mean()`, and add resampled aggregates as a stem or bar plot on a twin axis. For seasonality, `ax.axvspan()` highlights recurring intervals — weekends, quarters, fiscal years. Control date tick density with `mdates.MonthLocator()` and `mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y')` to prevent label overlap. Use `ax.fill_between()` to shade rolling standard deviation bands around the mean. For lag feature inspection, `ax.acorr()` plots autocorrelation directly — faster than building it manually. Set `maxlags` to limit computation. Combine all layers in subplots via `fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, sharex=True)` so zooming propagates across panels.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

dates = pd.date_range('2023-01-01', periods=365, freq='D')
ts = pd.Series(np.cumsum(np.random.randn(365)) + np.sin(np.arange(365)*2*np.pi/30)*5, index=dates)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, sharex=True, figsize=(12, 8))
axes[0].plot(ts, alpha=0.4, label='Raw')
axes[0].plot(ts.rolling(30).mean(), lw=2, label='30d MA')
axes[0].fill_between(ts.index, ts.rolling(30).mean()-ts.rolling(30).std(), ts.rolling(30).mean()+ts.rolling(30).std(), alpha=0.15)
axes[0].legend(); axes[0].set_title('Rolling Window')
axes[1].bar(ts.resample('ME').mean().index, ts.resample('ME').mean(), width=20, label='Monthly Mean')
axes[1].legend(); axes[1].set_title('Resampled')
axes[2].acorr(ts.values, maxlags=60, usevlines=True, lw=0.5)
axes[2].set_title('Autocorrelation (Lag Features)')
axes[2].xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

bytes.topic: Statistical Testing — hypothesis tests, p-values, confidence intervals, when they matter
bytes.title: matplotlib — Visualizing Hypothesis Tests with Confidence Intervals and P-Values
bytes.body: Statistical tests mean nothing if you can't communicate the result. matplotlib gives you precise control over plotting confidence intervals, shaded rejection regions, and annotated p-values directly on distribution curves. Use `ax.fill_between()` to shade alpha regions on a normal distribution, `ax.axvline()` to mark your test statistic, and `ax.annotate()` to label the p-value. For confidence intervals on means, `ax.errorbar()` accepts `xerr` or `yerr` arrays — pass your margin of error directly. When comparing groups, plot bootstrapped CI bars with `ax.bar()` plus `yerr` rather than relying on box plots that hide the test outcome. Key detail: `scipy.stats` returns the test statistic and p-value, but matplotlib is where you make it interpretable. Shade the area under the curve beyond your statistic using `fill_between` with a `where` mask. Add a legend distinguishing the rejection region from the confidence band. This turns a raw p=0.032 into a plot your team actually reads.
bytes.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 500)
y = stats.norm.pdf(x)
t_stat = 2.15

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, y, 'k-', lw=1.5)
ax.fill_between(x, y, where=(x >= 1.96), alpha=0.3, color='red', label='α=0.05 rejection')
ax.axvline(t_stat, color='blue', ls='--', lw=1.5, label=f't={t_stat}')
p_val = 2 * (1 - stats.norm.cdf(abs(t_stat)))
ax.annotate(f'p={p_val:.3f}', xy=(t_stat, 0.06), fontsize=10, color='blue')
ax.legend()
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Data Validation — schema validation with Pandera or Great Expectations, catching bad data early
bytes.title: Matplotlib — Validate Your Data Before Plotting with Pandera
bytes.body:
Bad data silently produces misleading charts. A single NaN in your series causes matplotlib to skip points without warning, and mixed-type columns trigger cryptic casting errors deep inside `ax.plot()`. Catch these issues before they hit your figure.

Pandera lets you define a schema contract for your DataFrame, then validate it as a pre-plotting step. Define expected dtypes, value ranges, and nullability constraints. When validation fails, you get a clear error instead of a broken axis or invisible data gap.

The pattern is simple: validate → plot. Wrap your plotting functions so they enforce the schema on input data. This is especially critical for automated reporting pipelines where matplotlib renders charts from upstream ETL outputs — you won't be eyeballing every figure.

Key checks for plotting data: no NaN in x/y columns, numeric dtype enforcement, monotonically increasing x-values for time series, and bounded ranges that match your `ax.set_xlim()`/`ax.set_ylim()` calls. Fail fast, plot clean.
bytes.code_snippet:
import pandera as pa
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

schema = pa.DataFrameSchema({
    "timestamp": pa.Column(float, pa.Check.increasing()),
    "cpu_usage": pa.Column(float, pa.Check.in_range(0, 100), nullable=False),
})

df = pd.DataFrame({"timestamp": [1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "cpu_usage": [55.2, 72.1, 68.4]})
validated = schema.validate(df)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(validated["timestamp"], validated["cpu_usage"])
ax.set_ylim(0, 100)
ax.set_ylabel("CPU %")
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Working with Large Data — chunking, Dask, sampling strategies, when to graduate to Spark
bytes.title: Matplotlib with Large Data — Downsample Before You Plot, Not After
bytes.body:
Matplotlib renders every single point you pass it. Feed it 10 million rows and it will dutifully create 10 million path segments, exhausting memory and producing an unreadable blob. The fix isn't Spark — it's smarter plotting.

First line of defense: **decimate before plotting.** Use `np.random.choice` or stride-based slicing (`data[::100]`) to reduce points to 5–50k. For time series, `lttb` (Largest Triangle Three Buckets) preserves visual shape far better than naive sampling.

For datasets that don't fit in memory, use Dask to lazily compute aggregates, then plot the summary. Dask's `.compute()` returns a pandas object matplotlib understands natively.

Use `rasterized=True` on heavy artists like `scatter()` or `pcolormesh()` — this renders them as bitmaps inside vector output, slashing PDF/SVG file size from 200MB to 2MB.

Finally, `plt.plot()` with `linewidth=0.5` and low alpha on downsampled data reveals density patterns better than plotting every point. Graduate to `datashader` or Spark only when aggregation itself won't fit in memory.
bytes.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Simulate large dataset (e.g., loaded via Dask then .compute())
x = np.linspace(0, 100, 10_000_000)
y = np.sin(x) + np.random.normal(0, 0.3, x.size)

# LTTB-style downsampling via stride
stride = max(1, len(x) // 20_000)
x_ds, y_ds = x[::stride], y[::stride]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x_ds, y_ds, linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.6, rasterized=True)
ax.set_title(f"10M points downsampled to {len(x_ds):,}")
fig.savefig("large_data.pdf", dpi=150)  # rasterized keeps PDF small
plt.show()

bytes.topic: Reproducibility — random seeds, environment pinning, notebook-to-script conversion
bytes.title: Matplotlib Reproducibility — Pinning Random Seeds for Consistent Plots
bytes.body:
Matplotlib delegates randomness to NumPy, but your plots still drift between runs when you forget to seed before data generation AND before layout operations. Two pain points bite hardest: jittered scatter plots and stochastic color cycling via colormaps sampled with np.random.

Pin reproducibility at three layers. First, seed NumPy before any data generation. Second, freeze your matplotlib rcParams so font resolution and figure sizing stay deterministic across machines — export them with matplotlib.rc_params().keys(). Third, when converting notebooks to scripts, replace plt.show() with fig.savefig() using explicit dpi and bbox_inches='tight' to avoid renderer-dependent bounding box differences.

The silent killer: matplotlib's Agg backend and interactive backends rasterize text differently, producing pixel-level drift in saved PNGs. Lock the backend with matplotlib.use('Agg') before any import of pyplot. Combine this with a requirements pin on matplotlib's exact patch version, since minor releases occasionally change default tick formatting and spacing algorithms.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')  # lock backend before pyplot import
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4), dpi=150)
ax.scatter(np.random.randn(200), np.random.randn(200),
           c=np.random.rand(200), cmap='viridis', alpha=0.7)
ax.set_title('Reproducible Scatter')
fig.savefig('plot.png', bbox_inches='tight', dpi=150)
print(f'matplotlib=={matplotlib.__version__}')  # pin this in requirements.txt

bytes.topic: Visualization Best Practices — choosing the right chart, color accessibility, avoiding chartjunk
bytes.title: matplotlib Visualization Best Practices — Charts, Color, and Cutting Chartjunk
bytes.body:
Choosing the right chart in matplotlib starts with your data's relationship type. Use `plt.bar()` for categorical comparisons, `plt.plot()` for trends over time, `plt.scatter()` for correlations, and `plt.hist()` for distributions — never substitute one for another just for aesthetics.

For color accessibility, avoid the default `tab10` cycle when distinguishing critical data. Use `plt.cm.viridis` or `plt.cm.cividis` for sequential data — both are perceptually uniform and colorblind-safe. For diverging data, prefer `RdBu` or `coolwarm`. Always validate with `matplotlib.colors.to_rgba()` if programmatically generating palettes.

Chartjunk in matplotlib is death by a thousand defaults: remove redundant spines with `ax.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)`, eliminate gridlines unless they aid reading via `ax.grid(False)`, and never use `plt.title()` alone — pair it with `ax.set_xlabel()` and `ax.set_ylabel()` for full context. Use `tight_layout()` or `constrained_layout=True` in `plt.subplots()` to prevent label clipping without manual padding hacks.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5), constrained_layout=True)

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
ax.plot(x, np.sin(x), color='#0072B2')  # colorblind-safe blue

# Remove chartjunk
ax.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(False)

ax.set_title('matplotlib — Sine Wave', fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('X', fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel('sin(X)', fontsize=11)

plt.show()

bytes.topic: Data Versioning — tracking dataset versions alongside model versions
bytes.title: matplotlib — Embedding Dataset Version Provenance Directly into Your Figures
bytes.body:
Matplotlib figures are portable artifacts — and `savefig(metadata={...})` lets them carry dataset provenance without a sidecar file. For PNG outputs, matplotlib writes arbitrary key-value pairs into iTXt chunks; for PDF, into XMP document info. Inject your DVC commit hash, S3 dataset URI, or LakeFS branch directly at save time, making the plot the canonical record of which data produced which curve.

For visual comparison across versions, overlay per-version learning curves on a single `Axes`. Use distinct `linestyle` values and version-tagged `label=` strings. Call `ax.axvline()` at the epoch boundary where a dataset was upgraded — this marks mid-run dataset transitions that would otherwise be invisible in a smoothed loss curve.

To automate provenance injection, shell out to `dvc params diff --show-md5` via `subprocess.check_output` and insert the result into the metadata dict before calling `savefig`. Later, verify embedded provenance with `PIL.Image.open('plot.png').text` — no log file required. This pattern turns every exported matplotlib figure into a queryable experiment record.
bytes.code_snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib
import numpy as np

epochs = np.arange(1, 21)
v1_loss = 0.8 * np.exp(-0.15 * epochs) + 0.05
v2_loss = 0.7 * np.exp(-0.18 * epochs) + 0.03

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.plot(epochs, v1_loss, label="dataset v1.2.0", linestyle="--", color="steelblue")
ax.plot(epochs, v2_loss, label="dataset v2.0.0", linestyle="-", color="tomato")
ax.axvline(x=10, color="gray", linestyle=":", label="dataset upgrade")
ax.set(xlabel="Epoch", ylabel="Val Loss", title="Val Loss by Dataset Version")
ax.legend()

fig.savefig(
    "training_curves.png",
    metadata={
        "dataset_v1_sha": "sha256:a3f9c1",
        "dataset_v2_sha": "sha256:b7c2e4",
        "model": "resnet50",
        "Software": f"matplotlib {matplotlib.__version__}",
    },
)
